name: Aspergillosis
notes: Chronic fungal infection of the respiratory tract; often seen in birds housed in dusty or poorly ventilated environments.

name: Avian Chlamydiosis (Psittacosis)
notes: Bacterial infection that can affect multiple organs and may be zoonotic; often presents with a combination of respiratory signs and general illness.

name: Bacterial Respiratory Infection
notes: Catch-all category for upper or lower respiratory tract infections caused by bacteria; severity can range from mild to life threatening.

name: Airsacculitis (Air Sac Disease)
notes: Inflammation or infection of the air sacs; often occurs together with other respiratory tract diseases.

name: Sinusitis / Rhinitis
notes: Inflammation of the upper airways and sinuses; can cause visible swelling around the eyes and nares.

name: Avian Influenza
notes: Highly contagious viral infection causing respiratory and systemic signs; certain strains can be zoonotic.
recommendedActions: isolate affected bird from any flock mates
seek urgent veterinary assessment
implement strict biosecurity and disinfection
avoid handling without appropriate protection

name: Psittacosis (Chlamydiosis)
notes: Chlamydial infection common in parrots and some other pet birds; important zoonotic disease.
recommendedActions: seek veterinary care promptly for testing and antibiotics
isolate the bird from people at higher risk (immunocompromised, elderly)
improve cage hygiene and air quality
follow veterinary guidance for long-term treatment and monitoring

name: Aspergillosis
notes: Fungal infection of the respiratory system, often associated with poor ventilation, dusty environments, or immunosuppression.
recommendedActions: schedule veterinary evaluation and diagnostic imaging if possible
improve ventilation and reduce dust and mold exposure
follow prescribed antifungal treatment
minimize stress and handle gently

name: Bacterial Pneumonia
notes: Lower respiratory tract infection that can develop secondary to other illness or poor environmental conditions.
recommendedActions: seek prompt veterinary examination and imaging
start appropriate antibiotic therapy as prescribed
maintain a warm, low-stress environment
monitor closely for worsening breathing effort

name: Upper Respiratory Infection
notes: Can be viral or bacterial in origin; often associated with environmental stress, drafts, or poor air quality.
recommendedActions: schedule veterinary visit to determine cause and treatment
keep the bird warm and away from drafts
improve air quality and cage hygiene
monitor for progression to more severe breathing difficulty

name: Hypocalcemia
notes: Low blood calcium, especially common in laying hens and some parrot species on imbalanced diets.
recommendedActions: seek veterinary evaluation and bloodwork
start calcium supplementation under veterinary guidance
transition to a balanced, species-appropriate diet
monitor for recurrence of neurologic signs

name: Vitamin A Deficiency
symptoms: keratinized debris around eyes and nostrils
notes: Common in birds fed seed-heavy diets without enough vitamin A–rich foods or formulated pellets.
recommendedActions: consult a veterinarian for diet assessment
switch to a balanced pelleted diet with fresh vitamin A–rich vegetables
treat secondary infections as needed
monitor skin, feather, and respiratory health over time

name: Egg Binding (Dystocia)
notes: Life-threatening condition where a bird is unable to pass an egg; requires immediate veterinary care.
recommendedActions: seek emergency veterinary care immediately
keep the bird warm and minimize handling
do not attempt to manually remove the egg at home
address underlying diet, calcium status, and environmental stressors

name: Obesity
symptoms: excess body fat on the keel and abdomen
notes: Common in pet birds on high-fat, seed-based diets with limited exercise opportunities.
recommendedActions: work with a veterinarian to design a gradual weight-loss plan
transition to a balanced pelleted diet with controlled treats
increase safe opportunities for flight or exercise
monitor weight regularly and adjust the plan as needed

name: Feather Plucking (Feather Destructive Behavior)
notes: Can be driven by medical issues (skin disease, pain, parasites) or behavioral causes such as stress, boredom, or improper environment.
recommendedActions: schedule veterinary exam to rule out medical causes
enrich the environment with toys and foraging opportunities
ensure adequate sleep and predictable routine
work with an avian vet or behaviorist on a long-term management plan
